title: Chicken Paprikash

overview.description: Tender chicken slowly cooked with onions and Hungarian paprika in a rich sauce finished with sour cream.

ingredients: ½ tsp hot Hungarian paprika, optional
method: Season the chicken thighs with salt and black pepper.
Heat the lard or oil in a large heavy-based casserole over a medium heat.
Brown the chicken thighs in batches, then remove them from the pan.
Add the onions to the pan and cook gently until very soft and lightly golden.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Remove the pan from the heat.
Stir in the sweet paprika and hot paprika if using.
Add a small splash of the chicken stock and stir to form a paste.
Return the pan to the heat.
Return the chicken to the casserole.
Pour in the remaining chicken stock.
Cover and cook gently for 40–50 minutes, until the chicken is tender and cooked through.
Mix the sour cream and flour together in a bowl.
Stir a few spoonfuls of the hot cooking liquid into the sour cream mixture to warm it gradually.
Stir the sour cream mixture into the casserole.
Heat gently for a few minutes until the sauce thickens slightly, but do not allow it to boil vigorously.
Scatter with fresh parsley before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Paprikás csirke is one of Hungary's best-known traditional dishes, and the word paprikás refers to dishes in which paprika is a defining ingredient.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to use paprika without burning it, slowly soften onions into a sauce and incorporate sour cream without curdling it.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple but deeply comforting Hungarian chicken dish that shows how a small number of carefully handled ingredients can create a rich and distinctive sauce.
chapman-tips: Use good-quality Hungarian paprika because it is the defining flavour of the dish.
Never let the paprika burn. Removing the pan from the heat before adding it is a simple way to avoid bitterness.
The onions should be properly soft before the paprika is added.
Use full-fat sour cream for the smoothest and richest sauce.
Temper the sour cream with hot cooking liquid before adding it to the pan to reduce the risk of curdling.
Nokedli is the classic accompaniment and is well worth making if you have time.
notes.batch-cooking: Cool completely before freezing in portions. Freeze the chicken and sauce together. The sour cream sauce may separate slightly when reheated, so reheat gently and stir well.
notes.leftovers: Remove the chicken from the bones and reheat gently with the sauce. Serve with noodles, potatoes or bread.
